summary: My great-grandmother, Karin Svenson Berg, was a remarkable woman who emigrated from Sweden in 1902. She was educated at the University of Lund and taught school in her hometown of Osby before coming to America. Though she was originally engaged to Andrew Berg, who sadly died before she arrived, she married his brother Nils after he invited her to come to Minnesota. Karin brought many cherished items with her, including gifts from her students and a basket box she carried on the boat, which have been passed down through our family.

Sweden is where my great-grandmother Karin Svenson Berg was born and raised. She lived in the town of Osby and was educated at the University of Lund before she emigrated in 1902. She carried with her many items from Sweden, including gifts from her schoolchildren and personal belongings, as she knew she might never return. Her journey from Sweden to America marked the beginning of our family's immigrant story.

Mora, Minnesota, was the first place my great-grandmother Karin Svenson Berg arrived at when she came to America. After traveling alone by steamship and train, she was met at the train depot in Mora by Nils Berg, who would become her husband. Mora was an important stop on her journey before they married at the Kanabec County Courthouse and moved on to live in Wahkon.

The University of Lund in Sweden is where my great-grandmother Karin Svenson Berg received her education. She was a trained teacher there before she taught school in Osby. Her education was a significant part of her identity and was recognized by the gifts she received from her grateful students before she left Sweden in 1902.

Osby was my great-grandmother Karin Svenson Berg's hometown in Sweden, where she taught school and was engaged to Andrew Berg. It was from Osby that she corresponded with Andrew and later Nils Berg, who invited her to America after Andrew's death. Osby represents the roots of our family story and the place she left behind in 1902.

Andrew Berg was my great-grandmother Karin Svenson Berg's first love and fiancé from Osby, Sweden. He had immigrated to America earlier with his brother Nils, and they corresponded while Karin was still in Sweden. Tragically, Andrew died in Minnesota before Karin could join him, but his memory remained with her throughout her life, as evidenced by a tiny box with his picture that she kept.

Nils Berg was Andrew Berg's brother who lived in Minnesota and played a pivotal role in my great-grandmother Karin Svenson Berg's life. After Andrew passed away, Nils found the letters between Andrew and Karin and invited her to come to America to marry him instead. Nils met Karin at the train depot in Mora, and they married at the Kanabec County Courthouse before settling in Wahkon, Minnesota.

Minnesota is where my great-grandmother Karin Svenson Berg's American story unfolded after she emigrated from Sweden in 1902. She first arrived in Mora, then married Nils Berg at the Kanabec County Courthouse, and finally settled in Wahkon, where they lived the rest of their lives. Minnesota became the new home for our family, shaping our history and identity.

The Kanabec County Courthouse is where my great-grandmother Karin Svenson Berg and Nils Berg were married after she arrived in Minnesota. This courthouse in Minnesota was the official place where their union was formalized before they traveled on horseback to their home in Wahkon. It holds a special place in our family history as the site of their marriage.

Wahkon, Minnesota, is where my great-grandmother Karin Svenson Berg and her husband Nils lived after their marriage. They traveled there on horseback from Mora, and it became their lifelong home. It is also where their only child, my grandfather Arthur Berg, was born in 1903, making Wahkon central to our family's roots in America.

Arthur Berg was the only child of my great-grandmother Karin Svenson Berg and Nils Berg, born in 1903 in Wahkon, Minnesota. He is my grandfather and the link between the immigrant generation and our family today. His birth marked the continuation of our family line in America after Karin's journey from Sweden.
